title: Wycinanki, layered Polish folk papercutting
subtitle: How to produce the traditional layered colour papercut from a folded template
excerpt: Wycinanki (pronounced vy-CHEE-nan-kee) is a Polish folk art in which layers of coloured paper are cut and stacked to produce decorative designs. The traditional form uses bold floral and bird motifs cut freehand from a folded sheet. The layering of contrasting colours gives the finished piece its characteristic brightness.
sourceNotes: Wycinanki as a documented folk tradition dates to at least the early 19th century in the Mazovia and Kurpie regions of Poland; ethnographic records of the technique appear in Polish folk-art surveys from the 1880s onward. The construction method described here follows the traditional layered approach documented in those accounts.

glossaryTerms.definition: Polish folk papercutting, traditionally produced using sheep shears or scissors on folded coloured paper. The word means 'cut-outs' in Polish. Characteristic motifs include roosters, flowers, and geometric star forms.

A papercutting technique in which multiple sheets of different colours are cut and stacked in sequence, each layer revealing a portion of the layer beneath. The layers create an impression of depth and colour variation in a flat object.

content.text: were cut freehand with sheep shears, the folk artist cut directly without a drawn template. The approach here uses a pencil template on a folded sheet, which produces the characteristic bilateral symmetry more reliably for a first attempt. The
technique requires at least two sheets of contrasting colours.

attrs.title: Craft knife safety
attrs.body: Always cut toward the edge away from your fingers, using a metal ruler to guide straight cuts. Keep the blade sharp, a dull blade requires more pressure and is more likely to slip. Replace blades regularly. Keep fingers behind the blade line at all times.

items.name: Coloured paper, 80-100 gsm
items.notes: Two or three strongly contrasting colours. Traditional wycinanki uses bright red, black, and white. The largest layer is the base; each subsequent layer is smaller.

items.name: Craft knife with fresh blade
items.notes: A sharp blade is essential for cutting fine details in the smaller inner layers.

items.name: Self-healing cutting mat, A4
items.notes: Place the folded sheet flat on the mat for all cuts.

items.name: Pencil
items.notes: For drawing the template on the folded sheet before cutting.

items.name: Metal ruler, 30 cm
items.notes: For straight cuts; many wycinanki motifs include clean geometric borders.

items.name: PVA, acid-free
items.notes: For adhering the layers together at the end.

content.text: Fold the base-colour sheet in half. Draw a half-profile of the intended motif on one half of the folded sheet, with the motif centre on the fold edge. Cut through both layers along the drawn outline, keeping the fold intact. When unfolded, the cut produces a bilaterally symmetrical silhouette. This is the largest and outermost layer of the finished piece.

Each subsequent layer is cut from a different colour and is smaller than the layer below, so that the lower colours show around the edges. Fold each colour sheet and draw an outline slightly inside the profile of the previous layer. The inner layers carry the decorative detail: interior cutaway shapes, petals, feather lines. Small interior cuts are made by piercing the paper with the knife tip and cutting outward.

Lay the layers in sequence, centring each on the one below. Apply a small dot of PVA to the back of each inner layer to hold it in position. Press flat under a weight for 15 minutes. Mount the finished
on a contrasting background sheet with a thin border of adhesive around the base layer only.
